item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. So this article is pretty self-contained. However, with the mention of O5-11 here, I feel an in-line link to the O5 Dossier could be made. There are six versions of O5-11 described there which turns the conversation with this skip from a fairly humorous spectacle to something rather creepy. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Addendum: Transcript of Interview between Jonathan Harris and O5-11 on 12-31-████

To this:

Addendum: Transcript of Interview between Jonathan Harris and O5-11 on 12-31-████

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Following up on past me: this SCP should be required reading for every new author, IMO. Proof that you can get away with as many cliches as possible if you make them work in context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspect that the blackboxes are disguising English rather than numbers (i.e. 'fifty' rather than '50').

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not having seen your wife for 10000 years is much weirder than not having seen your wife for 50 years. Scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1)I think this discussion is more interestimg then the SCP is. It's very… enlightening.

2)The thing I like in the SCP (other than breaking all the rules) is what isn't said, yet implied. Clearly breaking the contract would have servere consequences of an unkown nature, which is why the Foundation is willing to go along with it. It's the consequences that's anomalous, not John Harris or even the contract itself.

3)Just because a guy is old doesn't mean he can't enjoy video games. My dad is over 65 years old, has a Xbox-360 (admitidly which he uses mostly to watch Netflex) and has played a lot of video games on it (mostly Call of Duty and Borderlands 2). He also has a labtop. Thinking an old person wouldn't have a Game Console just becasue they're old is a bit prejudiced.

4)Really, this whole discussion is hillarious!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe it's Clef attempting to subvert/justify as many of the elements that compose a cliche bad humanoid skip, such as being granted whatever it wants, being allowed to interact with O5s, etc.
If you're confused about the anomaly itself, there's a contract concerning a guy named Jonathan Harris, and if the conditions aren't met some unspecified shit goes down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like i'm missing something here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, Jonathan Harris. That concession was rescinded as of 38 hours ago, per the Foundation's victory in the ensuing dice roll. Your food budget does not allow for such accommodations now.

:I Sorry Bruh.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I cannot choose between two comments, so I'll put in both.

1) I never have headaches. Except now. Thank you, Dr Clef.
However, since you are a doctor, could you prescribe something for me, please?

2) Dr Clef:
You are cat.
I am mouse.
I don't like it one bit.

Upvoted.

(PS Mr Harris' age comes in at four digits? Really? ;>)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there a term for a format screw that keeps the format intact? As in, something that goes against every single SCP writing convention while keeping "Item-#/Object Class/Special Containment Procedures/Description" intact?

I guess the term would be "style screw." And man, does it work well here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly don't know what to think about this one. I think I like it…. Im not sure

wait… just had a odd though… is the "humanoid." tab in http://www.scp-wiki.net/how-to-write-an-scp the contract? or do I just need more coffee (probably need more coffee)

item.title: Re: what?
item.content:encoded:
It's likely #3. For whatever reason, the Foundation has an anomalous contract that stipulates containing an otherwise normal person. If the Foundation breaches the contract, Something Bad/Anomalous Happens, and they don't want that to occur.

So, to contain the event, they need to follow that contract.

item.title: what?
item.content:encoded:
i have 2 explanations
1. saying the contracts anomaly is breaking the contract
2. the guy who wrote this thought "hey, all these articles are of things that are anomalous, but is the scp foundation really only for things that are anomalous and can have a few things that are just confidential?"
3. i am just missing something huge

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. It leaves a lot open to the imagination, and I can totally see this guy just devoting his life to being as big of a pain in the ass as possible for everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't it be both? An ego stroke attempt by way of writing a good SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you're doing here but I don't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article feels more like an ego stroke than an actual attempt to write a good skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a double standard because a veteran did something successfully that mist others can't, it's more like he's flaunting his skill. Those new writers usually get hounded so hard for doing something we tell them not to do but they do it anyway. Which always fails. Clef wrote this and it's still under quite a bit of scrutiny

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: When posting, didn't quite realize I was dogpiling on someone who had literally just taken leave for emotional reasons. My apologies.

Summary of previous post: I feel that statements that only staff could get away with such material, without being voted to -100 or deleted for trolling, serve to encourage people to stay within unnecessary boxes, and harm the wiki via encouraging stagnation and boredom. Therefore, these statements should be reconsidered.

The rest has already been said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fucking love this. Seriously, it's fantastic.

Yes, it's clearly omitting a lot of information. I don't see the Foundation withholding information in an SCP article as a problem, though; in another article this could have failed, but it works well for me here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I were staff, I would be voting on this as a troll article. Because that's what's happening: we're being trolled into thinking this is anomalous and well-written, when really if a new member of the wiki submitted it, we would be downvoting them so hard that they would hit -100 within a picosecond.

I'm very disappointed in you, ihpkmn. This is 1. Wildly inaccurate(See FlameShirt's comment for how people actually vote) and 2. Pretty insulting to the author, Clef, by saying he's only getting by on reputation, and everyone who upvoted this by implying that these people are enabling him by upvoting when they see his stuff, not because of quality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to mention he might just like to use it as a DVD player. It's not like he can use Netflix, hmmm?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mostly tales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
we're being trolled into thinking this is anomalous and well-written.
trolled into thinking this is anomalous and well-0written.
trolled into thinking this is well-written.
trolled
well-written

My mind is so full of fuck right now.

I guess lhpkmn believes that my name alone is enough to get lots of upvotes. I recommend he take a look at my SCP-2000 entry. That should educate him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My theory is that the old guy saw a commercial for the PS3 on TV (he does have access to modern TV programming), decided to get one out of morbid curiosity, and now it sits on his shelf unused and unloved.

Really it's to parody the whole "THIS GUY HAS A VIDYA GAME SYSTEM" part of the type of SCP this is based off of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand how this could be considered trolling, even in principle, unless you think that Clef literally possesses the power of hypnosis. If you're referring to him deliberately leaving out an element that's generally considered important and still getting +1's for it, that's not trolling, that's called being a skilled writer.

If you're just referring to your assertion that this would be immensely unpopular if a new user posted this, you are underestimating the site's culture of evaluating things w/o regard to their author, and overestimating the number of people who know and care who the author is before they vote on an SCP.

If you can't accept that people like something that you don't, feel free to stew in your own juices about it and argue about the article's merits, but refrain from accusations of trolling and from insulting the userbase, k?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to agree with others upthread that it would be a bit nice to understand what an old guy would want with a videogame system. I would also enjoy at least a hint at the nature of the contract and the consequences, but I still got the "Brick-shitting terror oh god we can't fuck this up" feeling from the article, and I thought it was cleverly written.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this before I looked at the comments or the author. Who wrote it didn't come into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, he's no longer classified as such. You're right that we don't know exactly what it does if the contract is violated, but you're wrong that Euclid classification puts much of an upper limit on how bad it is. As is (in my view over-) said, object class is based more on containment than danger. Though I doubt it's truly apocalyptic, we can logically rule out something beneficial by the fact that that would be a terrible incentive to keep him locked up.

Whatever it is, it is clearly something the Foundation wants to prevent badly enough to have an O5 naked and covered in blue paint. I similarly am skeptical of your conclusion that people are upvoting this because Clef. How could he even fool us into thinking something is well-written? That's not the sort of thing it's possible to employ duplicity about (save perhaps through plagiarism, theoretically).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly: O5-11 says he used to be called SCP-1949-A. So yes he is.

Secondly: We don't know what the contract says other than what's presented in the Containment procedures.

Thirdly: What anomalous goings-on?! We don't know what will happen if the contract is violated, and for all we know, the effect could be beneficial! Breaking the contract could mean that humanity never has to deal with cancer again, or that the poverty gap is suddenly eliminated, or that peace is declared worldwide for the next five decades! Whatever it is, it can't be that bad; it's Euclid, not Keter class.

If I were staff, I would be voting on this as a troll article. Because that's what's happening: we're being trolled into thinking this is anomalous and well-written, when really if a new member of the wiki submitted it, we would be downvoting them so hard that they would hit -100 within a picosecond.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First off, he's not an SCP. Second off, the O5s have zero contact with SCPs under any circumstances thing is a bit iffy. Thirdly, it's likely the contract specifies that only O5s count as sufficient to be a "Foundation representative". Even if this guy were an SCP (and the Foundation locks up more than just SCPs), if the Foundation has a choice between O5 having contact with an SCP and very bad anomalous goings-on, they'd probably choose the former.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. This all the way.

Look. I know we're not supposed to focus on the object, but on the story. And the story here is utterly uninteresting. It's just a guy tied to a piece of paper that says that the Foundation has to meet his every demand if he wins in a game of chance. Also, the last note makes no sense.

Jonathan Harris' advanced age and increasingly unhealthy diet (exacerbated by his mental state) have resulted in a marked decline in his health in the past year.

Advanced age? How old is he that he wanted a PS3 and a PC in his containment cell? It seems like the only purpose of this line is for Clef to say "No, it's okay that he has all this stuff, he's old and not a 20-something Mary Sue!" For that matter, it says that he can't have internet access, so why does he have a Kindle when the only way to get books on that is to access the internet?

Another thing that doesn't make sense is the fact that O5 met with a fucking SCP. I don't care if he's not anomalous, that's a big fat fucking breach of site canon. And you're all okay with it?

It seems like the only purpose of this article was to show that Clef could, in fact, write a successful SCP that had a name, access, could talk to O5s, access to a fucking PS3, and hold up a second. Didn't Story!Clef once help kill an SCP that had access to an Xbox? Isn't that a tad hypocritical? And furthermore: It's not fucking anomalous!

So, I guess the moral of this story is that so long as Clef or anyone who has been around for years on the wiki does these tropes, it's okay, but if a new person does them? Perish the thought and discourage them from ever posting again! Aren't double-standards wonderful?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Failure to provide Jonathan with monthly backrubs will result in demons overrunning Site 19 until such a time as Jonathan is provided with backrubs."

Contracts don't need to disintegrate completely the first time someone fails to follow the procedure laid out. You certainly don't need an anomaly to cause that. Hell, what would that even mean? A legal anomaly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what kind of contract reforms after a breech? the whole point of a contract is that a breech relieves both parties; unless that's part of the anomaly too, which seems thin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That falls under option 1: something happened, but it was left out of the article on purpose, for the purposes of lede-burying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are aware that you're ignoring a third option, and one which I find both the most likely and the one that justifies the Foundation's behavior here best: that the Foundation once assumed the contract was fake, didn't follow it, and suffered the exact consequences it denoted (since there's nothing saying the contract could only be broken once, it can easily be subjected to multiple failure states). In that case, the Foundation already knows that failure to follow the contract could lead to catastrophic results, hence their behavior here is reasonable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to concur with this. We have no information actually saying what will happen if the contract is broken. The only indication that this is anomalous is because the Foundation believes it to be anomalous: there is no actual proof.

Basically, this leaves me with the following options:

1) The lede, and the anomaly) is somewhere in the lower mantle of the Earth, which is too damn deep to justify for my tastes.
2) The Foundation is run by utter, absolute morons.

I know I hold the second option as my headcanon, but there are lines, and I consider that line to be crossed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough, but how is the contract anomalous? Other than telling us how to contain this guy, it doesn't seem anomalous. For all we know, it's just a high-up member of the site screwing with people by making the contract seem anomalous. What happens if the contract is broken? We're never told! It's not Keter-class, so it can't be world ending. Either tell us what will happen if the contract is broken, or I'm just going to assume that someone on the Ethics Committee made it up to see how the Foundation would react to an SCP that becomes gradually more humanized.

The contract isn't anomalous, the guy isn't anomalous… It's. Not. Anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're really curious, you can always check the recent contributions section of someone's wikidot user profile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know DrClef wrote this, which made me ask the question given his veteran status, but I was also wondering if DrBright still writes skips or tales anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The guy isn't anomalous. The contract is anomalous. We're containing the guy because that's what the contract says to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They do. Mann wrote the "Brain in a Jar" 2000 entry, and another one recently about an entity that appears to be following the rules of sone kind of cosmic board game (I wanna say 1848?).

This one was actually written by DrClef of SCP-231 fame. Gears may be on an extended LOA, but much of the site's "old guard" is still very active.

Speaking of which, I'm not a fan of this… :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…how the fuck is this anomalous? It's just a guy who can have stuff if he wins a game of luck. That's not anomalous. This shouldn't even be in containment, let alone be on the site.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stuff like this makes me wonder why veterans like Bright, Mann, or Gears don't write skips anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jonathan Harris isn't a skip.

The contract to contain him is the skip. You have to contain Jonathan Harris by the terms of the contract in order to contain the contract.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was really into the mystery and unanswered questions of this, until I reached the end and realised those questions were going to stay unanswered.

What a bummer for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, this is superb. Now if I could only figure out how X managed to make the Foundation sign the contract. It seems like a rather typical, if anomalous contract, so I would assume that it came from a rather typical, if anomalous transaction, which would mean that the Foundation would have gotten something out of the deal as well. But what could that be?…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohhhh. So the real skip here isn't the physical contract, but the contract itself. Fascinating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was a little toss-in to explain why everyone wants to give these guys 10m tall containment chambers… because it's three stories, of course! :D . . . right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent. I especially liked the huge cubic chamber.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The point is not that he breaches containment, the point that if he does, the Foundation would be in a breach of contract with X, and will experience corresponding penalties as outlined in 1949-Zulu. We (the readers) don't know what X is, and what sort of penalties it can enforce. What happens to Harris, the Foundation couldn't care less about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Contract signed by X to contain Jonathan Harris.

X-wife I presume?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aren't O5s never allowed to interact with SCPs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the best thing is that there's giant [DATA EXPUNGED]s slapped over the entire thing without a single one having actually been used. what the hell does 1949-zulu say??? how did we find this guy?? does he have any anomalous properties?? why are we containing him??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to mention having an O5 come into contact with a skip! You are truly a fiend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little convoluted, but I see why that is. Either way, I loved the hell out of the note at the end of the transcript.

The weird thing is… What's the worst that would happen if he breached containment? It's almost like we're containing him because we're containing him. To say nothing of whether or not the contract could be reworked for someone else after his death or something. Admittedly, that's sort of not the point, I'm just thinking out loud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Otherwise known as the School of Hard Knocks, AKA Life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Superbly executed. I wondered why this had such a high rating in such little time, but of course I quickly found out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really good. It literally dismembered the whole "you cannot do this in a humanoid" mentality we have. There's nothing against doing the things…they just can't be arbitrarily done. There has to be a reason and this article exemplifies that pretty well. Bravo on a job well-done Clef!

+1
